3: A direct revelation: the salvation announced by the Lord himself, attested by eyewitnesses, confirmed by signs and gifts of the Holy Spirit — this is the Trinitarian structure of the gospel's authentication. The Lord Jesus announced it; those who heard him testified; God confirmed it through the Spirit's distribution of gifts according to his will. The neglect of so great a salvation is therefore a neglect of the Triune God's own testimony about his Son.
9: A fulfillment: "we see him who for a little while was made lower than the angels, namely Jesus, crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death." The Psalm 8 vision of the human figure crowned with glory is fulfilled in the specific history of Jesus — the humiliation (crucifixion) and the exaltation (resurrection-crown). The general human destiny described in Ps 8 (dominion over creation) is actualized in the particular human being Jesus who, by tasting death for everyone, secures for all who are in him the Psalm 8 glory that was their created destiny. Creation's goal (human dominion) is achieved through the crucified and risen Son.
14: A direct revelation: through death he destroyed the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil, and delivered all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery. The Incarnation was purposive — not accidental but aimed at destroying the devil's death-dominion through participation in death itself. This is the paradox of the atonement: the one who is Life enters death to unmake it from within. The resurrection is not just the Son's personal vindication but the destruction of the enslaving power that death held over all humanity.
17: A type: the Aaronic high priest — chosen from among humans, offering for sins, able to sympathize because he himself is weak — is the OT institutional type whose characteristics are fulfilled and surpassed in Christ. The type reveals what the antitype will be: a priest who is genuinely human (made like his brothers in every respect), who propitiates for sins (makes atonement), and who can sympathize (merciful and faithful). But Christ exceeds the type in key dimensions: he is without sin (no self-offering needed), and his offering is himself (not animals). The type preserves the structure; the antitype perfects it.
1: A theme: the call to pay closest attention to what has been heard because the message is greater than the angels' message reflects the pattern of the entire OT-to-NT escalation that Christ's coming represents. Each warning passage in Hebrews is itself Christological: to neglect this salvation is to neglect the Son who announced it and the Father who confirmed it. The warning is addressed to those who have heard the Son's word and must now remain in it.
2: A theme: the law declared through angels was reliable and carried just recompense. This establishes the lower rung of the two-level argument: if the lesser (angelic/Mosaic) word carried binding force, how much more the word of the Son. The reliability of the Mosaic covenant's penalties is the argumentative basis for the severity of neglecting the Son's greater covenant. The law's authority serves as the measure against which the gospel's greater authority is calibrated.
4: A direct revelation: God confirmed the salvation-message through signs, wonders, miracles, and distributions of the Holy Spirit according to his will. The Trinitarian confirmation — Son announces, Father confirms, Spirit distributes — is the community's foundation for certainty. These confirmatory signs were the Spirit's testimony to the Son's identity and the Father's approval. The community that experienced these gifts stands on the ground of the Triune God's own attestation of the salvation in his Son.
5: A revelation of God: God did not subject the coming world to angels. This prepares the Christological argument: the world-to-come belongs to Christ, not to the angels. The age-to-come, for which the entire creation groans (Romans 8:22), is the domain of the Son's lordship. The angels governed aspects of the present age; the new creation is Christ's inheritance, the world into which he leads his people as their forerunner-pioneer.
6: A type: Psalm 8's "What is man?" establishes the pattern of human humiliation and exaltation over creation that finds its fulfillment in Christ. The Psalm begins with human smallness before the vast creation and ends with human dominion over it — a trajectory that describes both human vocation and the gap between vocation and reality. Christ is the one who closes this gap: the man who fully inhabits the human vocation of dominion-through-faithful-service.
7: A fulfillment: "You made him a little lower than the angels; you crowned him with glory and honor" — Christ's incarnation (made lower) and resurrection-exaltation (crowned with glory) fulfill the Psalm 8 trajectory. The LXX's "little lower" carries both a degree sense (slightly lower in status) and a temporal sense (for a little while) — both dimensions apply to the incarnation: the Son was genuinely lower in his human condition, and genuinely for a limited time. The crowning is the public event of the exaltation that reverses the humiliation.
8: A fulfillment (not yet complete): "You have put everything under his feet" — the Psalm 8 promise of universal dominion is already enacted in principle (at the resurrection) but not yet visibly complete ("at present we do not see everything subject to him"). This "already-not-yet" tension is the Christological structure of the present age: Christ reigns, but the reign is not yet universally acknowledged. The community lives between the "already" of the resurrection and the "not yet" of the parousia-consummation.
10: A direct revelation: it was fitting for God to make the pioneer of salvation perfect through suffering. The sufferings of Christ are not a theological problem to be explained away but the fitting and necessary path by which the Son became the completed, qualified, vocational high priest. The perfection through suffering is not moral improvement but the full traversal of the human path — from birth through death and into resurrection — that enables him to lead many sons and daughters to glory.
11: A direct revelation: the one who sanctifies and those who are sanctified are all from one Father, which is why Jesus is not ashamed to call them brothers and sisters. The shared origin in one Father is the basis of the family solidarity that defines salvation: the Son who sanctifies enters into genuine kinship with those he sanctifies. He is not ashamed of this solidarity even though it means identifying with the lowly, the failing, the mortal. This is the Christological heart of the atonement: real solidarity, real kinship, real brotherhood.
12: A fulfillment: the risen Christ fulfills Psalms 22:22 by proclaiming the Father's name to his brothers and sisters and singing praise in the midst of the assembly. The Psalm that begins with the cry of dereliction ("My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" — quoted at the cross) ends with the promise of praise in the assembly. The resurrection is the fulfillment of the Psalm's turn from abandonment to vindication; the risen Christ's proclamation of the Father's name in the community's worship is the enacted fulfillment of the Psalm's final movement.
13: A fulfillment: the two Isaiah 8 citations — "I will trust in him" and "Here am I and the children God has given me" — are fulfilled in Christ's own posture of trust toward the Father through suffering and in his identification with the community he redeems. Isaiah 8's righteous remnant who trusts while God hides his face is the OT pattern; Christ is the definitive fulfillment: the one who trusts the Father through the cross and presents to the Father the community entrusted to him.
15: A direct revelation: through his death Christ sets free those who all their lives were held in slavery by their fear of death. The liberation is comprehensive — not merely from death itself but from the enslaving anxiety that death-as-threat produces throughout human life. Christ's death destroys the mechanism by which the devil held humanity enslaved: the fear of death as the ultimate sanction loses its power when the one who entered death defeated it from within.
16: A fulfillment: it is not angels he helps, but Abraham's descendants — the Son's incarnation was targeted at the specific covenant community of Abraham's seed, fulfilling the Abrahamic promise by entering the family line it promised. The gospel's particularity (Jesus was a Jewish man from the tribe of Judah, born of a woman under the law) is the fulfillment strategy: the universal salvation comes through the particular covenantal trajectory of Abraham's seed. The heir of all things (Hebrews 1:2) comes as Abraham's descendant to fulfill what was promised to Abraham.
18: A direct revelation: because he suffered when he was tempted, he is able to help those who are being tempted. The incarnation gave Christ a permanent qualification: the tested high priest who knows temptation from within. This is not generic divine knowledge of human experience but the specific, earned qualification of the one who walked through every test. His help is not distant sympathy but the active aid of the one who has already been where those he helps are now.
